# LingClaw Workspace Instructions

This is **LingClaw** â€” a ~10000-line Rust personal AI assistant built on the **Skill + CLI** paradigm. Supports **OpenAI** and **Anthropic** APIs with auto-detection, including thinking/reasoning modes, plus experimental stdio MCP tool injection. Config via `~/.lingclaw/.lingclaw.json` (JSON config file) with first-run setup wizard.

**Skill** = LLM reasoning, system prompt, context management. **CLI** = Tool execution, safety, reliability. The entire project is one loop connecting them.

## Rules

- `src/main.rs` must stay â‰¤ 6000 lines. Check with `wc -l src/main.rs` before committing.
- Backend entrypoint, HTTP/WebSocket wiring, shared app types, top-level connection orchestration, and shared text utilities (`truncate()`, `tokenize_for_matching()`, `is_cjk_char()`) live in `src/main.rs`. ReAct state machine (`AgentPhase`, `AgentLoopCtx`, `FinishReason`, `evaluate_finish()`, `auto_think_level()`, `build_finish_nudge()`, `is_simple_query()`, observation summary, consecutive-error escalation via `build_observation_context_hint()`) lives in `src/agent.rs`. The phase-execution agent loop now lives in `src/runtime_loop.rs`, with socket idle/busy input helpers in `src/runtime_loop/socket_input.rs`; it uses `match react_ctx.phase()` to dispatch Analyze/Act/Observe/Finish phases, computes phase-adaptive think level (with user message complexity and consecutive error signals), drains pending interventions from the inbound channel, and uses `evaluate_finish()` for structured finish decisions, while the Act arm wraps long-running tools with progress heartbeats (`tool_progress`) and the shared `tool_timeout` budget and supports parallel execution for read-only tool batches via `is_read_only_tool()`, and the Observe arm debounces incremental session saves (5s interval; Finish always saves). The Analyze arm routes simple first-cycle queries to a cheaper `fast_model` when configured, injects a planning nudge on cycle 0, and appends `build_finish_nudge()` for deep loops. The Finish phase spawns a non-blocking post-execution reflection that appends to daily memory. Each run creates an isolated `CancellationToken` via `child_token()` tracked in `active_runs`; the `/stop` command or disconnect cancels the active run. Users can send text while the agent is busy â€” these are queued as deferred interventions and injected at the next Analyze phase boundary. CLI subcommands and setup wizard live in `src/cli.rs`. Provider streaming logic (OpenAI + Anthropic SSE, thinking blocks, conversation compression, usage tracking, prompt caching helpers, transient error retry via `send_with_retry()`) lives in `src/providers.rs`. Session prompt init/load logic, bootstrap baselines, daily memory, and local prompt composition live in `src/prompts.rs`; prompt templates live on disk in `docs/reference/templates/`. Shared tool registry lives in `src/tools/mod.rs` (includes `is_read_only_tool()` for parallel-execution gating) with implementations split into `src/tools/exec.rs`, `src/tools/fs.rs`, `src/tools/net.rs`, and the stdio MCP bridge in `src/tools/mcp.rs`. Configuration types (`Provider`, `Config`, `JsonConfig`, `JsonMcpServerConfig`, etc.) and loading logic live in `src/config.rs`. Token estimation, context budget, and message pruning live in `src/context.rs`. Hook system (`HookRegistry`, `AgentHook` trait, `HookInput`/`HookOutput`, `ToolHookInput`, `LlmHookInput`, `CommandHookInput`, `AutoCompressContextHook`, dispatch via `run_hooks()`, `run_tool_hooks()`, `run_llm_hooks()`, `run_command_hooks()`) lives in `src/hooks.rs`; hooks fire at 7 lifecycle points (`BeforeAnalyze`, `AfterObserve`, `OnFinish`, `BeforeToolExec`, `AfterToolExec`, `BeforeLlmCall`, `OnCommand`) with output-type validation per hook point. Tool hooks run sequentially in `execute_tool_call()`/`record_tool_result()`, in the parallel Act path via `HookEvalResult`, and in sub-agent tool execution in `src/subagents/executor.rs`; LLM hooks run in `run_analyze_phase()` with chained `extra_system`+`think_override` propagation and automatic re-prune when injection exceeds budget; command hooks run in `handle_idle_socket_input()` for all commands (including unknown) and via `fire_stop_command_hook()` (`tokio::spawn`) for non-blocking `/stop` hooks. Chat command handlers (`handle_command()`, `CommandResult`, per-command handlers) live in `src/commands.rs`. Sub-agent system lives in `src/subagents/` â€” `mod.rs` (registry types: `SubAgentSpec`, `ToolPermissions`, `AgentSource`, catalog rendering, tool filtering), `executor.rs` (isolated mini-ReAct loop with hook-integrated tool execution, context pruning via `message_budget_for_tool_defs()`, result streaming via parent `LiveTx`, per-cycle LLM channel forwarding tagged with `subagent` key, and config-driven model selection via `sub_agent_model`/primary fallback), `discovery.rs` (three-layer discovery: system `docs/reference/agents/`, global `~/.lingclaw/agents/`, session `{workspace}/agents/`, YAML frontmatter parsing with `parse_agent_frontmatter()`). The `task` tool in `src/tools/mod.rs` (`is_task_tool()`, `task_tool_definition_openai()`, `task_tool_definition_anthropic()`) is dynamically added to the runtime tool set when agents are discovered; execution flows through `execute_task_tool()` in `src/runtime_loop.rs` which is the single source of `task_started`/`task_completed`/`task_failed` terminal events. Agent definitions use `AGENT.md` files (YAML frontmatter with `name`, `description`, `max_turns`, `tools.allow`/`tools.deny`, plus Markdown body as system prompt; legacy `model` fields are ignored). Built-in agents live in `docs/reference/agents/` (explore, researcher, coder). Structured async memory (schema, storage, debounced LLM extraction queue with incremental merge via `merge_llm_response_into_memory()`, `prefilter_for_memory()` for lightweight message excerpts, query-aware injection via `format_memory_for_injection()`, `/memory` status) lives in `src/memory.rs`. Global usage statistics live in `src/session_admin.rs`. Session persistence (with .tmp crash recovery on load) and migration live in `src/session_store.rs`. WebSocket session payload delivery and command refresh helpers live in `src/socket_sync.rs`. WebSocket reader/writer task setup lives in `src/socket_tasks.rs`.
- LingClaw runs a single persistent session (`MAIN_SESSION_ID = "main"`) with its workspace at `~/.lingclaw/main/workspace/`, containing 7 prompt files (BOOTSTRAP.md, AGENTS.md, IDENTITY.md, SOUL.md, USER.md, TOOLS.md, MEMORY.md) copied from `docs/reference/templates/` on creation, plus `memory/` for daily logs and `skills/` for installable skill modules. The session records the initial IDENTITY.md and USER.md content as bootstrap baselines. Bootstrap mode loads `BOOTSTRAP.md + AGENTS.md`; once either IDENTITY.md or USER.md differs from that session baseline, the backend auto-removes `BOOTSTRAP.md` and normal mode loads `AGENTS.md + IDENTITY.md + USER.md + SOUL.md`, then the session's `MEMORY.md` and today/yesterday daily memory. Connection takeover: when a new browser connects, the old connection is cancelled via `connection_cancels`, any active agent run is stopped via `SessionRunBinding` in `active_runs`, and `LiveRoundState.connection_id` tracks ownership for safe cleanup. Skills are loaded from three layers: system (`docs/reference/skills/`), global (`~/.lingclaw/skills/`), and session (`skills/`) â€” later sources shadow earlier ones on name collision. System skill directories support recursive discovery for org folders (e.g. `docs/reference/skills/anthropics/pdf/SKILL.md`). Skill metadata (name + source + description) is injected into the system prompt each round; system skills can be disabled via `disabled_system_skills` (a `HashSet<String>` stored in Session, persisted with `#[serde(default, skip_serializing_if)]`). `/skills-system` supports `install`/`uninstall` subcommands for runtime enable/disable of system skills by pattern (e.g. `anthropics` for all, `anthropics/pdf` for one); installing a sub-skill when a parent group is disabled expands the parent into sibling-level disables. Existing sessions should not recreate BOOTSTRAP.md on reconnect.
- `/new` only compresses conversation, appends to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`, and clears context. It must not initialize a new session or recreate `BOOTSTRAP.md`.
- Dependencies: axum, tokio, serde, serde_json, reqwest, futures, regex, tower-http, tokio-util, chrono, base64, getrandom. Do not add more without justification.
- Rust edition 2024. Target stable Rust.
- Use `cargo clippy` and `cargo fmt` before finalizing code.
- **Mandatory code review**: After completing any code change, a code review must be performed before committing. Review scope: correctness, security (OWASP Top 10), style compliance, error handling, and test coverage. Run `cargo test` and `cargo clippy` as part of the review. No commit without review.
- Frontend (`static/index.html`) does not count toward the line budget.
- Config via `~/.lingclaw/.lingclaw.json` (JSON config file) with first-run setup wizard. Environment variables are supported as fallback overrides (e.g. `LINGCLAW_OPENAI_STREAM_INCLUDE_USAGE`, `LINGCLAW_ANTHROPIC_PROMPT_CACHING`, `LINGCLAW_TOOL_TIMEOUT`, `LINGCLAW_STRUCTURED_MEMORY`, `LINGCLAW_FAST_MODEL`, `LINGCLAW_SUB_AGENT_MODEL`).
- Experimental MCP support is configured via the top-level `mcpServers` object in `~/.lingclaw/.lingclaw.json`. Only tool injection is supported today. Stdio transport uses newline-delimited JSON-RPC with the current MCP protocol version and handles server-initiated requests such as `ping`. Exposed MCP tool names are prefixed with `mcp__`; configured server `cwd` values must stay inside the current session workspace; default MCP request timeout inherits `toolTimeout` unless `timeoutSecs` overrides it. Startup `start` / `restart` run a bounded one-shot MCP preflight, and `mcp-check` provides deeper diagnostics with the configured runtime timeouts without reusing cached runtime MCP sessions.
- Provider-specific features use compatibility gating: official API endpoints (e.g. `api.openai.com`, `api.anthropic.com`) auto-enable features; third-party compatible gateways default to disabled but can be force-enabled via config or env var.
- Default HTTP port is `18989` (`DEFAULT_PORT` in `src/main.rs`). Linux install/start helpers live in `src/cli.rs`, and `scripts/install-linux.sh` is the documented Linux install flow.
- `lingclaw doctor` checks install readiness: Rust toolchain (>= 1.85.0 for edition 2024), Cargo, Git, platform tooling (PowerShell on Windows, systemd/nohup on Linux), source-vs-installed version, source-vs-remote version. Offers auto-fix prompts for fixable issues. Implementation: `handle_doctor_command()` and helpers (`parse_version_triple()`, `detect_rustc_version()`, `detect_cargo_version()`, `detect_git_version()`, `read_local_source_version()`) in `src/cli.rs`.
- Model resolution must support both `provider/model` and plain model IDs. For plain IDs, prefer an exact match to the current runtime config, then same-provider candidates, with deterministic ordering.
- No `.unwrap()` in production paths â€” use `?` or provide fallback defaults.
- All tool exec must go through `check_dangerous_command()`. User-supplied tool paths must go through `resolve_path_checked()`; internal sandboxed path normalization uses `resolve_path()`.
- Network tools must go through `check_ssrf()` (async; scheme validation + private IP / `spawn_blocking` DNS resolution blocking) with redirects disabled.
- Unit tests live under `src/tests/`. Each module's tests go in `src/tests/<module>_tests.rs`, included via `#[cfg(test)] #[path = "tests/<module>_tests.rs"] mod <module>_tests;` at the bottom of the corresponding source file. Do not put inline `#[cfg(test)] mod tests { ... }` blocks in production source files.

## Extending

- **New tool** â†’ Add a `ToolSpec` entry in `src/tools/mod.rs` + parameter builder + handler wrapper. Put the implementation in `src/tools/fs.rs`, `src/tools/net.rs`, or `src/tools/exec.rs` by category. If the work is specifically about MCP-discovered tools or stdio server integration, update `src/tools/mcp.rs` and related config/docs instead of the built-in `ToolSpec` registry. This is CLI-side work.
- **New provider** â†’ Add SSE parsing + streaming in `src/providers.rs`, add variant to `Provider` enum in `src/config.rs`. This is Skill-side work.
- **Better reasoning** â†’ Improve `build_system_prompt()` in `src/main.rs`, `prune_messages()` in `src/context.rs`, or edit prompt templates in `docs/reference/templates/`. For agent decision layer changes (phase transitions, finish heuristic, observation summary), edit `src/agent.rs`. This is Skill-side work.
- **New command** â†’ Add handler function and match arm in `handle_command()` in `src/commands.rs`. This is Commands-side work.
- **New CLI subcommand** â†’ Add match arm in `handle_cli_command()` in `src/cli.rs`. CLI subcommands run inside `#[tokio::main]` (multi-threaded runtime is active) but before the Axum server starts; they return early via `return`. Do not use `unsafe` APIs that require single-threaded execution (e.g. `std::env::set_var`). Use `--serve` for foreground mode.
- **Config change** â†’ Update `JsonConfig` / `JsonSettings` / `JsonMcpServerConfig` structs + `Config::load()` in `src/config.rs` + `run_setup_wizard()` in `src/cli.rs` if interactive. Add env var override in the `Config::load()` env-var block if the setting needs runtime override, and keep README/example config aligned when changing `toolTimeout`, `mcpServers`, `structuredMemory`, or related runtime settings.
- **Session prompt change** â†’ Edit template files in `docs/reference/templates/`, or modify `init_session_prompt_files()` / `load_session_prompt_files_with_snapshot()` in `src/prompts.rs`. This is Skill-side work.
- **New skill** â†’ Place `skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (YAML frontmatter with `name` + `description`, plus instruction body) in any of the three skill layers: `docs/reference/skills/` (system), `~/.lingclaw/skills/` (global), or a session workspace `skills/` (session). Skills are auto-discovered from all three layers at each prompt round and merged with session > global > system precedence. System skill directories support org-level nesting (e.g. `docs/reference/skills/anthropics/pdf/SKILL.md`): directories without a SKILL.md are recursed into automatically. Skill discovery logic lives in `src/prompts.rs` (`SkillSource`, `discover_all_skills()`, `discover_skills_by_source()`, `discover_skills_in_dir()`, `parse_skill_frontmatter()`, `render_skills_catalog()`, `is_system_skill_disabled()`, `list_system_skill_groups()`). SKILL.md format is compatible with the [Agent Skills spec](https://agentskills.io).
- **New sub-agent** â†’ Place `agents/<name>/AGENT.md` (YAML frontmatter with `name`, `description`, `max_turns`, `tools.allow`/`tools.deny`, plus Markdown body as system prompt) in any of the three agent layers: `docs/reference/agents/` (system), `~/.lingclaw/agents/` (global), or a session workspace `agents/` (session). Agents are auto-discovered via `discover_all_agents()` in `src/subagents/discovery.rs` and merged with session > global > system precedence. The `task` tool is dynamically registered when agents exist. Sub-agent execution is a mini-ReAct loop with isolated message history, filtered tools, hook-integrated tool execution (BeforeToolExec/AfterToolExec via `run_tool_hooks()`), and result streaming. All sub-agents use `agents.defaults.model.sub-agent` when configured, otherwise the primary model; legacy `model` fields in AGENT.md are ignored. `ToolPermissions.is_allowed()` always blocks the `task` tool to prevent recursive delegation. AGENT.md format follows the skill discovery pattern.
